content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


January 11, 1994

Richard C. Smith
Nations Credit Political
Action Committee
1105 Hamilton Street
Allentown, PA 18101

Re: Your Request For Informal
Assistance
Our File No. I-93-404

Dear Mr. Smith:

You have requested advice regarding the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ Because your request is
a general inquiry, we treat it as one for informal assistance
pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).2/

QUESTIONS

1. Is it permissible for an out-of-state political action
committee ("PAC") to make contributions to statewide candidates in
California or a state PAC domiciled in California?

2. Assuming contributions are permissible, what reports are
required?

3. Are there any laws prohibiting contributions to
candidates for an office that regulates licensed financial
services companies?

4. Does California prohibit PAC disbursements to candidates
when the corporation pays the administrative expenses of the PAC?


1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Richard C. Smith
Our File No. I-93-404
- Page 2


CONCLUSIONS

1. The Act does not prohibit an out-of-state PAC from making
contributions to candidates in California or to PACs domiciled in
California. However, the out-of-state PAC may incur reporting
requirements under the Act.

2. If an out-of-state PAC receives contributions totaling
$1,000 or more in a calendar year for the purpose of influencing
the voters to vote for or against specific candidates or ballot
measures in California, it will qualify as a "recipient committee" --
under the Act and will incur campaign disclosure obligations.
Enclosed are informational materials as well as the appropriate
forms.

3. The Act does not prohibit contributions to candidates for
state offices which regulate licensed financial companies.
However, you also may want to consult federal law on this issue.

4. The Act does not prohibit corporate contributions.
Therefore, PAC disbursements to candidates are not prohibited if a
corporation pays the administrative expenses of the PAC.

ANALYSIS

An out-of-state PAC which receives funds for purposes other
than making contributions to or expenditures on behalf of
California state and local candidates, ballot measures or
committees has no filing obligations if that PAC qualifies as a
"committee" under California law.

Section 82013 defines "committee" as any person or
combination of persons who does any of the following:

(a) Receives contributions totaling one thousand
dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year; or

(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year; or

(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand
dollars ($10,000) or more in a calendar year to or at
the behest of candidates or committees.

The term "contribution" is defined in Section 82015 as a
payment made for political purposes. A payment is made for
political purposes if it is made "for the purpose of influencing
the action of the voters for or against
the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the
qualification or passage of any measure...'" or is received by or
made at the behest of a candidate or a committee. (Regulation
18215.)

Richard C. Smith
Our File No. I-93-404
Page 3


A contribution also includes any monetary or non-monetary
payment that is received by an organization which is not formed or
existing primarily for political purposes (e.g., an out-of-state
PAC) if the payment is "earmarked" for the making of contributions
or expenditures. A payment is "earmarked" when, at the time of
making the payment, the donor knows or has reason to know that the
payment or funds with which the payment will be commingled will be
used to make contributions to California candidates or committees.
(Regulation 18215(c).)

The Commission has advised that an organization which has not
notified its contributors that it will support or oppose
California state or local candidates or committees, will qualify
as a recipient committee under Section 82013(a) during the second
year in which it engages in such activities. That is because any
contributions made during the first calendar year in which the committee engages in such activities establish a
"history" that puts the organization's contributors on notice that
some or all of their funds may be used for political purposes. If
the organization makes contributions or other expenditures for
political purposes totaling $1,000 or more during any subsequent
calendar year, it is deemed to have "received" contributions
totaling $1,000 or more.

During the first year in which such an organization makes
contributions or expenditures in California, it will be required
to file independent expenditure committee reports if it makes
independent expenditures totaling $1,000 or more to support or
oppose California candidates or measures, or major donor committee
reports if it makes contributions to California state or local
candidates or committees totaling $10,000 or more.

Enclosed is an information sheet which describes how
organizations like yours may qualify as committees under the Act.
Also enclosed are informational manuals which outline the
reporting requirements of recipient committees, independent
expenditure committees and major donor committees, as well as the
appropriate disclosure forms, and a copy of the "Political Reform
Act as of 1974 as Amended to January 1, 1993." If you determine
that your organization will incur filing obligations during 1994,
please contact this office to obtain the 1994 materials.

I hope this information will be helpful to you. If you have
any additional questions, please contact me at (916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division

Enclosures
